citation: Bourgon V, Duval F-G, Forest G. Circadian athletic variations when traveling in the Canadian Football League. Chronobiology International. 2026. DOI: 10.1080/07420528.2026.2637795
abstract: This study investigated circadian disadvantage related to travel direction and longitudinal distance in the Canadian Football League (CFL) using 8 years (2014–2021) of regular season evening game data (n=387 away games). One-way ANOVAs and linear regression analyses were applied across three travel direction categories (westward, same time zone, eastward) for team, offensive, and defensive performance variables. Results showed that westward travel was associated with worse score differentials and time of possession, and that greater longitudinal distance traveled westward predicted poorer defensive performance, while eastward travel was associated with performance improvements.
tldr: CFL teams traveling westward for evening games perform significantly worse than those traveling eastward or within the same time zone. Defensive variables were most sensitive to circadian disruption from westward travel, while offensive variables were largely unaffected except total first downs. Performance decline scales with longitudinal distance traveled westward. Staff should prioritize circadian management strategies — light exposure, sleep scheduling, and travel planning — especially when teams face long westward trips to evening games.
methods: Retrospective analysis of 387 CFL regular season away evening games (after 18:00 local time) from 2014 to 2021 (pre-COVID). Data extracted from footballdb.com and cfl.com. Variables categorized as team (score differential, time of possession, penalties), offensive (first downs, yards per play, pass completion, interceptions, sacks, fumbles, kickoff/punt returns, field goal %), and defensive (same metrics allowed). Travel direction (westward, same time zone, eastward) and longitudinal distance traveled were the independent variables. One-way ANOVAs with LSD post hoc t-tests and linear regression analyses were conducted.
findings: Significant effects of travel direction on score differential (F[2,286]=3.86, p=0.022, η²=.06) and time of possession (F[2,286]=5.42, p=0.005, η²=.09). Post hoc tests confirmed westward travelers performed worse on both variables. Defensive variables significantly affected by travel direction included average yards allowed per play (p=0.008), average yards allowed per passing play (p=0.005), and average kickoff return yards allowed (p=0.035). Regression analyses confirmed that greater longitudinal distance traveled westward significantly predicted worse score differential (β=.13, p<.001), less time of possession (β=2.06, p<.001), fewer first downs made (β=.03, p=.004), and worse defensive metrics including yards allowed per play (β=-.01, p<.001) and first downs allowed (β=-.04, p=.003).
limitations: Data were observational and derived from publicly available sources, limiting causal inference. Circadian phase was inferred from travel direction and time zone crossings rather than direct biological measures (melatonin, core body temperature, actigraphy). Performance metrics were assessed at the team level, potentially masking individual variability. Travel duration, sleep quality, injuries, and lineup changes were not controlled. Data limited to 2014–2021.
practicalImplications: NFL and CFL teams should incorporate structured circadian management protocols for westward-traveling teams playing evening games. Interventions such as controlled light exposure, strategic sleep scheduling, phase-advancing strategies, and careful travel planning can help mitigate circadian disadvantages. The finding that eastward travel may provide a circadian advantage suggests scheduling considerations for game times and travel logistics could offer a meaningful competitive edge.
athleteDev: Player development programs should educate athletes on chronobiology and circadian rhythm management as a performance factor. The differential impact on defensive versus offensive play suggests reactive skill development (defensive reads, pass coverage, decision speed) may be especially vulnerable to circadian disruption, making circadian optimization strategies particularly important for defensive players during road trips.
rtp: Return-to-play protocols for CFL and NFL athletes should account for circadian disruption from travel, particularly westward travel for evening games. Clinicians assessing readiness should consider that reactive, cognitively demanding tasks (pass coverage, pursuit angles) may be more impaired by travel fatigue than pre-planned offensive sequences. Scheduling return-to-participation assessments at the same time of day as upcoming games helps ensure accurate functional readiness evaluation.

citation: Smith RS, Guilleminault C, Efron B. Circadian Rhythms and Enhanced Athletic Performance in the National Football League. Sleep. 1997;20(5):362-365.
abstract: This retrospective study examined 25 years (1970–1994) of Monday Night Football (MNF) games between West Coast (WC) and East Coast (EC) NFL teams to test the hypothesis that circadian timing confers an athletic advantage. WC teams playing at 9:00 p.m. EST are effectively competing at 6:00 p.m. their local time (closer to peak performance), while EC teams play near their biological nadir. Logistic regression controlling for point spreads and home-field advantage showed that WC teams won significantly more often and by more points than EC teams, outperforming Las Vegas point spread predictions by a statistically significant margin.
tldr: West Coast NFL teams hold a significant circadian performance advantage over East Coast teams during Monday Night Football, winning 63.5% of games and outperforming Las Vegas point spread predictions in 67.9% of cases. This advantage is comparable to or greater than home-field advantage in magnitude. EC teams playing at 9:00 p.m. EST are performing near their biological nadir, while WC teams are competing around their physiological peak (6:00 p.m. local time). Football staff should treat game time relative to home time zone as a meaningful performance variable, not just travel logistics.
methods: Retrospective data analysis of all 63 MNF games involving EC versus WC NFL teams from the 1970–1994 seasons (25 seasons) with 9:00 p.m. EST start times. EC teams defined as those in the Eastern Standard time zone; WC teams as those in the Pacific time zone. Win-loss records, point margins, and performance relative to Las Vegas point spreads were examined using logistic regression with home-field advantage as an indicator variable. Two logistic regressions were run: one with home games as the indicator and one also including point spread as a predictor. All-time home and away winning percentages since 1970 were calculated and compared to MNF performance.
findings: WC teams won 63.5% of games versus EC teams' 36.5%, winning by an average of 14.7 points per game compared to EC's 9.0 points. WC teams outperformed Las Vegas point spread predictions at p<0.01 in both logistic regressions. Simply picking the WC team predicted the winner against the spread 67.9% of the time. WC teams' MNF home winning % was 71% (vs. their 59.3% all-time home rate); EC teams' MNF home winning % was 43.8% (vs. their 56.5% all-time). Overall record difference between WC and EC was only 4.4 percentage points, far less than the 27-point MNF gap.
limitations: Retrospective study unable to determine exact circadian phase of each athlete at game time. Travel strategies of teams changed over the 25-year study window. Individual variation in circadian adjustment to time zone changes could not be assessed. Point spread data from 1970–1972 (7 games) could not be located and were excluded from the point-spread regression. Confounding variables, though largely nullified by the point spread analysis, could not be completely eliminated.
practicalImplications: NFL staff should treat game time relative to home time zone as a critical performance variable. EC teams playing MNF against WC opponents should consider phase-delaying circadian clocks by 3 hours in the days prior to the game. WC teams should maintain home-zone scheduling when traveling east. The circadian effect appears at least as impactful as home-field advantage, suggesting sleep and circadian management is a high-ROI intervention for performance staff.
athleteDev: Circadian rhythm education should be integrated into athlete development programs. Training times influence biological peak performance timing, and athletes who consistently train in the late afternoon may benefit from scheduling practices aligned with anticipated game times. For developing players, establishing consistent sleep-wake schedules tied to competition times can optimize performance readiness over the long season.
rtp: Circadian considerations are relevant when scheduling return-to-play progression for injured athletes. Rehabilitation sessions and functional testing should ideally be conducted at the same time of day as typical game times to ensure assessments reflect true game-ready performance. Athletes returning from injury during road trips should have travel-related circadian disruption factored into clearance decisions.

citation: Bourgon V, Duval FG, Forest G. Circadian variation in coaches' decision-making in the National Football League's evening games. Chronobiology International. 2024. DOI: 10.1080/07420528.2024.2393880
abstract: This study examined whether circadian misalignment from westward travel affects NFL coaches' fourth-down decision-making across 20 seasons (2000–2020, n=5360 games). Factorial ANCOVA and moderation analyses tested whether travel direction and longitudinal distance interacted with game time to predict offensive play-calling on fourth down. Coaches on westward-traveling teams called significantly more offensive fourth-down plays (14.90%) during evening games compared to same-time-zone (12.20%) and eastward-traveling (11.35%) teams, an effect absent in daytime games, suggesting circadian misalignment impairs coaching cognition.
tldr: NFL coaches traveling westward for evening games call significantly more aggressive (risky) fourth-down plays than coaches traveling eastward or within the same time zone — an effect that disappears entirely for daytime games. This is the first study to show that circadian misalignment extends beyond player physical performance to coaching decision-making. The effect scales with westward longitudinal distance and only emerges in games starting after approximately 16:24h. Sleep deprivation and circadian disruption from westward travel may compromise in-game cognitive judgment, and protocols to mitigate this (sleep interventions, phase-shifting) should extend to coaching staff.
methods: Retrospective analysis of 5360 regular season NFL visiting team games from 2000–2020 (pre-COVID), excluding international games and playoff games. Data extracted from profootballreference.com and nfl.com. Variables included travel direction (westward, same time zone, eastward), longitudinal degrees traveled (−51° to +51°), time of game (day <18:00h vs. evening ≥18:00h, and as continuous variable), year of season, and percentage of offensive plays on fourth down. A factorial ANCOVA (Time of game × Direction of travel) with year as covariate was conducted, followed by post hoc ANOVAs and Tukey t-tests for day (n=4410) and evening (n=950) games separately. A moderation analysis tested whether time of game moderated the relationship between longitudinal degrees traveled and fourth-down play calling.
findings: Significant interaction between Time of game and Direction of travel (F[2,5358]=5.39, p=0.005). In evening games, westward-traveling teams had 14.90% offensive fourth-down plays vs. 12.20% (same time zone, p=0.03) and 11.35% (eastward, p=0.007). No significant direction effect in daytime games (p=0.62). Moderation analysis: significant longitudinal degrees × time of game interaction (F[1,5355]=9.51, p=0.002). Westward longitudinal degrees significantly predicted greater fourth-down aggression only for games starting at 16:24h or later. The explained variance was modest (0.2%) but statistically significant and meaningful in the context of elite competition.
limitations: No data on coaches' individual sleep patterns or chronotypes. Game situational variables (score, time remaining, field position, opponent strength) were unavailable and could not be controlled. Fourth-down decisions represent only one of many coaching decisions per game and may be influenced by cumulative in-game context. The study was exploratory with a modest effect size. Causal inference is limited by the retrospective observational design.
practicalImplications: Sleep and circadian health protocols should extend beyond players to include all coaching and support staff. Teams traveling westward for evening games should implement pre-travel phase-shifting strategies (morning bright light, evening melatonin) for coaches as well as players. NFL schedulers should consider offering earlier kickoff times to teams traveling west across multiple time zones. Organizations should monitor in-game decision metrics in relation to travel schedule as part of performance management.
athleteDev: This research broadens the scope of circadian science in football to include coaching staff cognitive performance. For player development, it underscores the importance of preparing players for complex decision-making demands (quarterbacks, defensive captains) under conditions of circadian disruption, particularly when preparing for westward road trips with evening kickoffs.
rtp: From a return-to-play perspective, this study highlights that cognitive readiness — not just physical readiness — must be considered when clearing athletes after travel-heavy schedules. Medical and performance staff should monitor decision-making quality and cognitive load tolerance in players returning from injury during periods of circadian disruption, particularly in westward-travel evening game scenarios.

citation: Mack C, Matava M, Zeidler K, Sills A, Solomon G. Clinical Research in the National Football League: The Player Scientific and Medical Research Protocol. Current Sports Medicine Reports. 2020;19(5):168-174.
abstract: This invited commentary describes the NFL and NFLPA's jointly developed Medical Research Approval Process (MRAP), a structured, multilevel governance framework governing the use of NFL player workplace injury and illness data for clinical research. Established as a side letter to the 2011 CBA (effective January 26, 2015), MRAP outlines seven distinct research pathways based on the requester's affiliation, research intent, and data sensitivity. The protocol ensures research feasibility, protects player confidentiality, avoids duplication of effort, and requires sequential approvals from IQVIA, NFL legal, the NFL Chief Medical Officer, NFLPA, and IRBs where applicable.
tldr: The NFL and NFLPA jointly created the Medical Research Approval Process (MRAP) to govern how player injury and illness data can be used for research. Seven pathways cover everything from internal club use to third-party publications, each with a specific approval chain. All research using NFL EMR data must go through this system. Key points for staff: research using NFL player data requires multi-level institutional approval; publicly available injury data (news reports, injury reports) are unreliable as primary sources; and any research project, even internally focused, must follow MRAP to avoid sanctions including committee removal and club discipline.
methods: This is an invited commentary and descriptive article outlining the MRAP governance structure. The authors describe the seven sequential research pathways (I: Interventional; II: Internal Club EMR; III: Medical Committee De-identified; IV: Medical Committee Identifiable; V: EMR Research for Publication; VI: Case Study for Publication; VII: Third-Party Data Request), the approval parties required at each pathway, and the process for initiating, reviewing, and disseminating research under the MRAP framework. No experimental design or statistical methods are employed.
findings: MRAP consists of seven distinct pathways governing NFL medical research by type of requester, data sensitivity, and intended use. All research requires sequential approval; denial at any stage terminates the request. The protocol explicitly states the NFL and NFLPA may offer nonbinding comments but have no editorial control over publications. Publicly sourced injury data are identified as unreliable, particularly for lower-grade injuries not resulting in missed playing time. MRAP-governed EMR data linked with game statistics and player demographics provides the most reliable injury incidence and outcome data. Since 2019, individual clubs have access to injury dashboards comparing their data to league averages including return-to-play metrics.
limitations: As a descriptive commentary, no empirical limitations apply. MRAP is specific to the NFL-NFLPA collectively bargained environment, which may limit direct generalizability to other sports leagues or non-unionized athletic contexts. The article does not provide data on the volume or outcomes of research proposals reviewed through MRAP.
practicalImplications: NFL team physicians, athletic trainers, and performance staff who wish to conduct or publish research on player data must route all requests through the MRAP system via the NFL MRAP web portal. Research proposals require feasibility review by IQVIA and approval from NFL management council legal, the NFL Chief Medical Officer, and NFL EVP of Health and Safety. Drafts must be submitted 60 days before journal submission and 30 days before final publication. Teams should avoid using publicly available injury data as primary sources due to systematic underreporting of lower-grade injuries.
athleteDev: The MRAP framework enables systematic, high-quality research on NFL player health across the career arc, including injury prevention, performance outcomes post-surgery, and long-term health. Access to comprehensive, curated EMR-linked game statistics allows researchers to study position-specific injury patterns, recovery trajectories, and performance-based return-to-sport outcomes at a scale not possible with public data.
rtp: MRAP is directly relevant to return-to-play research quality. Studies using MRAP-approved NFL EMR data capture the full spectrum of injuries — including lower-grade injuries not reported publicly — providing more accurate incidence rates and RTP timelines. Practitioners should prioritize research published through MRAP-governed data channels over studies relying on publicly sourced injury reports when setting evidence-based return-to-play benchmarks for NFL players.

citation: Assef N, Endsley C, Skoumal C, Schlafke J, Dewald M. Closed Kinetic Chain Upper Extremity Stability Test Reference Values for NCAA American Football. International Journal of Sports Physical Therapy. 2025;20(9):1338-1344. doi:10.26603/001c.143374
abstract: This cross-sectional study established CKCUEST reference values for 80 male NCAA football players (Divisions 1, 2, and 3) from three institutions, accounting for position, injury history, year in school, and division. Players averaged 27.3 touches with a minimal detectable change of 1.2 touches (clinically, 2 touches). Linemen (offensive and defensive combined) averaged significantly fewer touches (25.7) than all other positions combined (28.1, p<0.01), and linebackers averaged more touches (29.7) than offensive linemen (24.8, p=0.03). No significant differences were found based on NCAA division, year in school, or history of upper extremity injury.
tldr: NCAA American football players averaged 27 touches on the CKCUEST, with a position-based distinction: linemen score significantly lower (~25.7) than all other positions (~28.1). A change of at least 2 touches represents a clinically meaningful difference. Prior upper extremity injury did not reduce scores, supporting use of this test as an objective RTP benchmark regardless of injury history. Staff should apply position-specific reference values rather than a single universal cutoff when using the CKCUEST for return-to-sport clearance in football.
methods: Cross-sectional study approved by three university IRBs with data collected spring/summer 2024. Eighty male NCAA football players (Division 1 n=21, Division 2 n=37, Division 3 n=22) completed three 15-second CKCUEST trials (push-up position, hands 36 inches apart, alternating hand touches for maximum repetitions) with 45-second rest between trials; trials were averaged for the score. Independent variables: position, height, mass, BMI, year in school, upper extremity injury history. Comparisons used one-way ANOVA with Bonferroni post hoc corrections, independent t-tests for linemen vs. all other positions, and Pearson correlation coefficients for continuous anthropometric variables. MDC95 calculated from SEM.
findings: Mean touches: 27.3 (95% CI 26.4–28.1). MDC: 1.2 touches (2 touches needed for clinically meaningful change). Linebackers (29.7) vs. offensive linemen (24.8): p=0.03. Linemen as a group (25.7) vs. all other positions (28.1): p<0.01. No significant correlations between touches and height (r=−0.02), mass (r=−0.18), BMI (r=−0.20), year in school (r=0.16), or division (r=0.12). No significant difference between players with (26.6) and without (27.6) upper extremity injury history (p=0.30). No significant differences by NCAA division (Division 1: 26.0, Division 2: 28.0, Division 3: 27.2).
limitations: Data collected at three sites with different researchers, introducing potential inter-rater variability (mitigated by standardized video instructions and phone follow-up). Different floor surfaces across facilities may have influenced touch counts. Some positions had small sample sizes, limiting statistical power for pairwise position comparisons. Injury history was self-reported and not differentiated by injury type or severity.
practicalImplications: A reference value of 27 touches should be used as a benchmark for NCAA football players on the CKCUEST. A change of at least 2 touches represents a clinically meaningful difference. Position-specific norms should be applied: linemen (OL and DL) typically score around 25–26 touches, while non-lineman positions average 28+ touches. Staff should not use a single universal benchmark for return-to-sport clearance without accounting for positional demands. Pre-season CKCUEST scores below 21 touches have been previously associated with elevated in-season shoulder injury risk.
athleteDev: The CKCUEST is a practical, low-equipment upper extremity functional assessment suitable for pre-season screening of all football position groups. Establishing position-specific baseline scores at the start of each season allows staff to track changes in upper extremity stability and identify at-risk athletes. Linemen's lower normative scores (25.7) reflect their unique positional demands and higher body mass, and development programs for linemen should incorporate closed-chain upper extremity strength and stability training to optimize performance on this metric.
rtp: The CKCUEST with a football-specific reference value of 27 touches (or position-adjusted: ~25.7 for linemen, ~28.1 for non-linemen) provides a standardized, validated benchmark for upper extremity return-to-sport clearance following shoulder, elbow, or wrist injuries in NCAA and NFL-level football players. A minimum of 2 touches improvement from post-injury baseline is needed to confirm measurable recovery. Prior upper extremity injury history did not reduce CKCUEST performance, supporting its use as an objective clearance criterion regardless of injury background.
